message.content:
Adversarial cryptography reviewer: REFUTE this candidate; default refuted=true unless solid. Read the real cb-mpc code at C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc first.

CANDIDATE: Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor
api: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h
class: Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container severity: Medium
file: src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183
missing_check: After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().
data_flow: decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).
attacker_role: Malicious party that produces the batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via the public decrypt_batch API.
impact: A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.
excerpt: // pve_batch.h convert(): pre-check is a no-op, then convert(Q,...) resizes Q to attacker count
if (int(Q.size()) != n) { converter.set_error(); return; }
converter.convert(Q, L, b);
// pve_batch.cpp restore_from_decrypted: loops to n, indexes attacker-sized Q
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { MODULO(q) x[i] = x0[i] + x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i] * G) return coinbase::error(E_CRYPTO); }

LENS = CONCRETE BOUNDARY ATTACK. Construct the exact malicious-party deviation through the public API and the concrete impact (which secret/forgery/DoS). Honest party runs unmodified code. If no plausible attack, refuted=true.

is_real=true only if it survives against the real code. Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now let me look at the converter's vector overload to see if it resizes the destination vector.

This is the critical piece. The candidate's core claim is confirmed at the converter level: the `std::vector<T>` overload at lines 114-131 does `value.clear()` then `value.resize(count)` with the attacker-controlled `count`. So during deserialization, `Q` will be resized to the attacker's inner length prefix `m`, NOT to `n`.

And the pre-check in `pve_batch.h::convert` (`if (int(Q.size()) != n)`) runs BEFORE `converter.convert(Q, ...)`, so it's checking the constructor-sized vector (size `n`) — a no-op as the candidate claims.

Now I need to verify the data flow: how `decrypt_batch` constructs the `ec_pve_batch_t` and whether `n` (the batch_count used in the loop) is independent of `Q.size()`. Let me read the actual decrypt path.

Now I have the full picture. There's a critical guard I need to examine: after `convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)`, there's a loop at line 165-167 that iterates `pve_ct.get_Qs()` and calls `q.get_curve()`. Let me trace this carefully.

The key question is: after deserialization, `Q.size() == m` (attacker-controlled, possibly `< n`), but `n` (the loop bound in `restore_from_decrypted` and `decrypt`) stays as `blob.batch_count`. Let me verify whether the deserialization actually completes successfully when `Q.size() != n`, and check whether `convert` rejects it via the strict trailing-bytes check or otherwise.

Let me re-examine the `convert` flow within `ec_pve_batch_t::convert`:

1. Pre-check: `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` — Q is currently `n` (constructor), so this passes (no-op).
2. `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` — this calls the variadic, which first does `convert(Q)`. The vector overload clears Q, reads length prefix `count = m`, resizes Q to `m`, reads `m` points. Then `convert(L)`, `convert(b)`.
3. Then the kappa rows loop.

So if the blob is internally consistent (length prefix `m` for Q, and the rest of the bytes match what the deserializer expects for `m` points + L + b + rows), deserialization succeeds with `Q.size() == m != n`. The strict trailing-bytes check in `deser` doesn't apply here because `coinbase::convert(T&, mem_t)` (line 276-282) does NOT do the trailing-bytes check — only `deser` does. Let me confirm which one is used: line 163 uses `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` which is the template at line 276. That does NOT reject trailing bytes. 

Now the curve check loop at 165-167 iterates `get_Qs()` which is the `m`-sized vector — it only validates the `m` present entries, so it does not catch the mismatch. The candidate correctly notes this.

Let me now examine `restore_from_decrypted` and the actual OOB. The loop at line 181 `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)` reads `Q[i]` where `Q.size() == m < n`. This is `std::vector::operator[]` without bounds check — genuine OOB read. Let me verify `ecc_point_t` and its `operator!=` to confirm the wild pointer deref.

Now there's a crucial detail. The candidate claims OOB read on `Q[i]` accessing an `ecc_point_t` whose embedded `EC_POINT*` is "uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory." But there's an important subtlety I must check: `std::vector<ecc_point_t>::operator[]` with `i >= size()` reads beyond the vector's allocated buffer. This is genuinely undefined behavior / OOB read.

However, I need to carefully verify whether the attack reaches line 183 at all. The candidate's data flow claims that `base_pke.decrypt` returns SUCCESS for some row, and then `restore_from_decrypted` is called. Let me trace more carefully whether `restore_from_decrypted` can actually reach the `Q[i]` loop with `Q.size() < n`.

Let me look at the rows. In `restore_from_decrypted`, before the `Q[i]` loop, there are these checks: `row.x_bin.size() != n*curve_size`, `r01.size != 16`, `x1_bin.size != n*curve_size`. Then `bn_t::vector_from_bin(..., n, ...)` for x0 and x1 — both produce size-`n` vectors. Then the loop reads `x0[i]`, `x1[i]` (both size n, fine) and `Q[i]` (size m < n, OOB).

So the OOB is real **if** the attacker can get one of the kappa `base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf)` to return SUCCESS and pass the size checks. Let me examine the base PKE decrypt to understand whether an attacker (who only knows the public `ek`, not `dk`) can craft a ciphertext `rows[i].c` that decrypts successfully under the honest party's `dk`.

content.text:
The detail about whether `base_pke.decrypt` returns SUCCESS is actually not essential to the OOB claim. Even if no row decrypts successfully, the loop at line 198-202 in `decrypt` just `continue`s and eventually returns E_CRYPTO. But the candidate claims the attacker CAN make a row decrypt. Even more importantly — let me reconsider: even if a row decrypts and we reach `restore_from_decrypted`, the OOB only triggers if we reach the `for(int i=0;i<n;i++)` loop.

But wait — there is a much more fundamental question. Let me re-examine the **pre-check no-op claim** very carefully, because this is the linchpin. Let me re-read lines 48-61 of pve_batch.h.

The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` happens with Q at constructor size n — passes. Then `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` resizes Q to m. So yes, the check is a no-op for catching the mismatch. This is confirmed.

Now, the truly decisive question for a **read** OOB: does `std::vector<ecc_point_t>::operator[](i)` for `i >= size()` actually dereference a wild `EC_POINT*`? Let me check the `operator!=` / `operator==` implementation.

This confirms the deref path. `Q[i] != x[i]*G` calls `ecc_point_t::operator!=` on the OOB `Q[i]`. The operator reads `this->ptr`, `this->curve`, then calls `curve.ptr->equ_points(...)`. If `Q[i]` is past the vector's allocation, `this->ptr` and `this->curve` are whatever garbage memory holds — could be nullptr (returns early, no crash but logic), or a non-null wild pointer leading to `curve.ptr->equ_points` dereferencing wild memory (crash / wild EC_POINT deref).

Now the most important question is **reachability** of the `restore_from_decrypted` `Q[i]` loop. This requires `base_pke.decrypt` to return SUCCESS for at least one row. Let me check whether the attacker, who has the public `ek` but not `dk`, can craft a ciphertext `rows[i].c` that decrypts successfully. Let me look at the base PKE decrypt.

`genPVELabelWithPoint` hashes the m-sized Q (whatever it is) — no iteration to n, no crash there. So the inner_label is just `sha256(serialized m-point Q)`. The attacker controls Q and label, so they can compute this inner_label and encrypt valid rows under it. Confirmed.

Now let me verify the `vector_from_bin` for x0 and x1 produces size-n vectors (so `x0[i]`, `x1[i]` for i<n are safe and only `Q[i]` is OOB). Let me check `bn_t::vector_from_bin`.

Confirmed: `vector_from_bin(..., n, ...)` resizes `out` to `n`. So `x0` and `x1` are both size n. In `restore_from_decrypted`, the loop `for (i=0; i<n; i++)` accesses `x0[i]`, `x1[i]` (size n - fine), `x[i]` (xs, sized to n by decrypt's `xs.resize(n)` - fine), and `Q[i]` (size m < n - **OOB**).

Now I have a near-complete picture confirming the candidate. Let me double check whether there's a git history / patch that already added validation that I might be missing, and check the test file to understand intended behavior. Let me also verify the constructor's `Q.resize(n)` is the only place Q gets its initial size and confirm `convert` is the deserialization entry. Let me check if there's possibly a length consistency check I missed — e.g., does the row deserialization or something else cross-check n.

Let me look at whether the `convert` re-check (`if (int(Q.size()) != n)`) was perhaps intended to run AFTER. Let me check git blame / recent changes to this file.

content.text: Only one commit (shallow clone). Let me verify the claim about whether `convert`'s pre-check could be intended as a post-check, by examining how other similar deserialization in the codebase validates vector sizes. Let me look at the non-batch `ec_pve_t::convert` for comparison, which is the established pattern.

In the single (non-batch) `ec_pve_t`, Q is a single `ecc_point_t` (not a vector), so there's no length-mismatch issue there. The batch version is where the vector `Q` introduces the risk.

Now I'm confident about the structural facts. Let me do one final critical check: confirm that the build is a release/non-asserting build for `std::vector::operator[]` (it always is unchecked unless `_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS` / hardening is on), and check whether there's a global `cb_assert` or hardening that would catch it. Let me check whether they compile with `_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS` or libc++ hardening.

29	  set_cxx_flags("-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2")
30	endif()
31

33	  set_cxx_flags("-Wno-tautological-undefined-compare")
34	  set_cxx_flags("-Wno-#pragma-messages")
35	  set_cxx_flags("-Wno-vla-extension")
36	  set_cxx_flags("-Wno-error=deprecated-declarations")

45	endif()

50	endif()

53	# In particular, `-Wl,--exclude-libs,ALL` breaks ASAN+UBSAN builds (gtest crashes
54	# during test registration with a bad-free). Skip it for sanitizer builds so we
55	# can run sanitizer lanes in CI and locally.
56	set(_cbmpc_is_sanitized false)
57	if(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR CMAKE_C_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR
58	   CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR CMAKE_SHARED_LINKER_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=")

content.text:
No `_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS` or libc++ hardening that would turn `std::vector::operator[]` into a bounds-checked operation. `_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` does not cover `std::vector::operator[]` (it's for libc functions like memcpy/sprintf). So in a standard release build, `Q[i]` for `i >= size()` is a genuine, unchecked OOB read. Confirmed.

Now let me reconsider the entire candidate critically as the adversarial reviewer. The structural facts all hold:

1. ✅ Converter's `std::vector` overload does `clear()` + `resize(count)` with attacker count.
2. ✅ The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` in `ec_pve_batch_t::convert` runs BEFORE `convert(Q,...)` while Q is still constructor-sized n — genuine no-op.
3. ✅ `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` does not enforce trailing-bytes (only `deser` does), but actually the blob can be fully consistent anyway.
4. ✅ The curve-check loop only iterates the m present Q entries — doesn't catch mismatch.
5. ✅ decrypt path runs `skip_verify=true`, so `verify()` (which enforces `Q.size()==n`) is skipped.
6. ✅ `restore_from_decrypted` validates row_index but not Q.size(); loops `i<n` reading `Q[i]`.
7. ✅ `ecc_point_t::operator!=` dereferences `ptr`/`curve` from OOB memory → wild EC_POINT deref / crash.
8. ✅ Reachability requires one base-PKE row to decrypt successfully; attacker holding public ek CAN craft a valid row under the attacker-controlled inner_label.

Let me scrutinize point 8 more — the genuine question of whether reaching `restore_from_decrypted` is necessary, and whether `m < n` with a fully-consistent blob is constructible. Let me reconsider: actually, even simpler reachability. The OOB happens at `restore_from_decrypted` line 183. But before that, line 159/168 check `row.x_bin.size() != n*curve_size` or `x1_bin.size != n*curve_size`. These use `n` (the honest batch_count). The attacker controls `row.x_bin` (it's in the serialized blob). So the attacker sets `x_bin.size == n*curve_size` to pass. Fine.

But also note: the attacker doesn't even strictly need a SUCCESSFUL pke decrypt to be cryptographically meaningful — they just need `base_pke.decrypt` to return SUCCESS (no error) so `restore_from_decrypted` is invoked. For RSA-OAEP, a validly-formed OAEP ciphertext under the public ek decrypts to whatever plaintext the attacker chose. So yes, fully reachable.

Let me reconsider whether there is any even-easier crash. Actually, the candidate notes `m can be < n`. But what if `m > n`? Then `Q[i]` for i<n is in-bounds. So the attack specifically needs `m < n`. With `m < n` and a consistent blob (m points serialized for Q, then L, b, and kappa rows with x_bin sized n*curve_size), deserialization succeeds, Q.size()=m<n. Confirmed constructible.

Let me verify there is no validation in the c_api layer or higher that re-checks. Let me check the c_api decrypt_batch wrapper.

No additional validation at the c_api layer. The `catch (...)` blocks catch C++ exceptions but NOT a segfault from dereferencing a wild pointer (OOB read of an `ecc_point_t` struct that has a non-null garbage `ptr`/`curve`, then calling `curve.ptr->equ_points`). A SIGSEGV is not a C++ exception, so the catch-all does not help; it crashes the process.

I've now thoroughly validated every link in the chain. Let me make sure I'm not missing a defense by checking whether `verify` is somehow still invoked. In `ec_pve_batch_t::decrypt`, line 193: `if (!skip_verify && (rv = verify(...)))`. With `skip_verify=true`, verify is short-circuited. So `verify`'s `Q.size()==n` check (line 78) is never reached on the decrypt path. Confirmed.

One more consideration as adversarial reviewer: is the `m < n` actually constructible such that deserialization of the rows succeeds? The rows are kappa fixed-count rows (kappa = SEC_P_COM, a compile-time constant). The convert reads exactly kappa rows after Q, L, b. The attacker fully controls the blob bytes, so they construct: length-prefix m, m serialized points, L buf, b (16 bytes), then kappa rows each with (x_bin, r, c). All attacker-controlled. The `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` doesn't enforce trailing bytes, but the attacker can make it exact anyway. Fully constructible.

Let me verify `kappa`/`SEC_P_COM` value to confirm it's a fixed compile-time count (not attacker-controlled, which is fine — rows aren't the issue).

kappa = 128, fixed. Good.

I have now fully verified the candidate against the real code. Let me summarize my adversarial assessment.

The candidate is structurally accurate on every claim I could verify:

1. **The no-op pre-check** (`if (int(Q.size()) != n)` before `convert(Q,...)`) — CONFIRMED. Q is constructor-sized n at that point; the check passes, then `convert(Q,...)` overwrites Q's size.

2. **The converter resizes Q to attacker count** — CONFIRMED at `convert.h:114-131`: `value.clear()` then `value.resize(count)` where count is the attacker's length prefix (bounded only by `MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS = 1<<20`, which permits any `m < n`).

3. **`n` stays = `blob.batch_count`** (a separate outer field), decoupled from `Q.size()` — CONFIRMED at `pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159,162`.

4. **decrypt runs with `skip_verify=true`, bypassing the only `Q.size()==n` check (in `verify`)** — CONFIRMED at `pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174` and `pve_batch.cpp:193`.

5. **The curve-check loop only iterates the m present Q entries** — CONFIRMED at `pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167`.

6. **`restore_from_decrypted` loops `for(i=0;i<n;i++)` reading `Q[i]` with `Q.size()==m<n`** — CONFIRMED at `pve_batch.cpp:181-184`. `x0`/`x1`/`xs` are all sized n (safe); only `Q` is short → unchecked `std::vector::operator[]` OOB read.

7. **The OOB element's `ecc_point_t` is dereferenced** via `operator!=` → `curve.ptr->equ_points(*this, val)` reading garbage `ptr`/`curve` — CONFIRMED at `base_ecc.cpp:909-917`.

8. **Reachability**: requires `base_pke.decrypt` to return SUCCESS for ≥1 of the 128 rows. The base PKE is RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256; the attacker holds the public `ek` and controls `Q`+`label`, so can compute `inner_label` and forge a validly-decrypting row. ACHIEVABLE.

9. **No stdlib hardening** (`_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS`/libc++ hardening absent; `_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` doesn't cover `vector::operator[]`), and the `catch(...)` at the c_api layer doesn't catch SIGSEGV. So the OOB is a real, exploitable memory-safety defect (crash/DoS minimum).

Minor inaccuracy in the candidate: the impact claim that `get_public_keys_compressed_batch` / `get_Label_batch` "share the same construct+deserialize pattern" and are equally vulnerable is **wrong** — those paths only iterate the m-sized `get_Qs()` and never index to n, so they do NOT trigger the OOB. Only the `decrypt_batch` family (including the HSM variants) does. This doesn't refute the core finding; it's an overstatement of the affected surface.

Severity: a remotely-triggerable OOB read of an `ecc_point_t` followed by a wild pointer dereference in OpenSSL, reachable purely through the public API by a malicious counterparty supplying the ciphertext blob. This is a genuine memory-safety bug. Medium is a reasonable-to-conservative rating (DoS/crash certain; controlled read possible depending on heap layout; no secret disclosure).

input.reason:
Verified against the real cb-mpc code; every load-bearing claim holds.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed): converter_t's std::vector overload (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h:114-131) does value.clear() then value.resize(count) using the attacker's length prefix (bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20). In ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) the guard `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` runs BEFORE converter.convert(Q,L,b) while Q is still constructor-sized n (pve_batch.h:13 Q.resize(n)). So the guard is a genuine no-op, and convert() then sets Q.size() = attacker count m. The outer loop bound n stays = blob.batch_count, a SEPARATE field (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159,162), decoupled from Q.size().

BYPASS OF EXISTING CHECKS (confirmed): the only Q.size()==n enforcement is in verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78), but decrypt_batch calls decrypt with skip_verify=true (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174 -> pve_batch.cpp:193 short-circuits verify). The post-deser curve loop (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) only iterates the m present get_Qs() entries, so it does not detect m<n. coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce the strict trailing-byte check that deser() has, and the blob is fully attacker-constructible anyway (length-prefix m, m points, L, b, then kappa=128 fixed rows).

OOB + DEREF (confirmed): restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops for(i=0;i<n;i++) and reads Q[i] while Q.size()==m<n. x0/x1 (bn_t::vector_from_bin resizes to n, base_bn.cpp:547) and xs (resize(n)) are all size n and safe; only Q is short, so Q[i] is an unchecked std::vector::operator[] OOB read of an ecc_point_t. ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917) reads this->ptr/this->curve from the OOB struct and calls curve.ptr->equ_points(...), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT*/curve pointer.

REACHABILITY (confirmed achievable): reaching restore_from_decrypted requires base_pke.decrypt to return SUCCESS for >=1 of the 128 rows (pve_batch.cpp:200). The base PKE is RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256 (pve_base_pke.cpp); the malicious party holds the PUBLIC ek and controls both Q and label, so it can compute inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q) (sha256 of attacker's m-point Q) and forge a validly-decrypting row. No stdlib hardening turns operator[] into a checked access (compilation_flags.cmake: no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS/libc++ hardening; _FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 does not cover vector::operator[]), and the c_api catch(...) (c_api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp) does not catch a SIGSEGV.

IMPACT: a remotely-triggerable OOB read + wild EC_POINT* dereference on the honest decryptor, reachable purely through the public decrypt_batch / decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm API by a malicious counterparty supplying the ciphertext blob. Crash/DoS is certain; controlled OOB read is plausible depending on heap layout. No honest secret is leaked.

MINOR OVERSTATEMENT (does not refute): the candidate lists get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch as equally vulnerable. They are NOT — they only iterate the m-sized get_Qs() and never index to n, so they cannot trigger the OOB. Only the decrypt_batch family is exploitable. Core finding stands.
input.attack_at_boundary: Deserialization trust boundary: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (and decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm) consuming the untrusted serialized pve_batch ciphertext blob supplied by a malicious counterparty. The malicious-party deviation: serialize ec_pve_batch_t with an inner Q length-prefix m strictly less than the outer batch_count n, with all 128 rows present and x_bin sized n*curve_size, and at least one row.c a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek and inner_label=sha256(serialized m-point Q). The honest decryptor (skip_verify=true) deserializes Q to size m, decrypts that row, enters restore_from_decrypted, and reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 (OOB), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT* inside ecc_point_t::operator!=.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial review. The candidate survives against the real cb-mpc code.

Summary of my verification:

The bug is real. Every load-bearing claim checks out against the actual source:

- The "no-op pre-check" at `include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61` runs while `Q` is still constructor-sized `n`, then `converter.convert(Q,...)` overwrites `Q` via the `std::vector` overload in `convert.h:114-131` (`clear()` + `resize(attacker_count)`).
- The outer loop bound `n` comes from a separate field `blob.batch_count` (`pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159,162`), decoupled from `Q.size()`.
- The only `Q.size()==n` enforcement lives in `verify()` (`pve_batch.cpp:78`), which `decrypt_batch` deliberately skips via `skip_verify=true`.
- `restore_from_decrypted` (`pve_batch.cpp:181-184`) reads `Q[i]` for `i<n` while `Q.size()==m<n` — an unchecked `std::vector::operator[]` OOB read of an `ecc_point_t`, then dereferenced in `operator!=` -> `curve.ptr->equ_points` (`base_ecc.cpp:909-917`).
- Reachability is achievable: the attacker holds the public `ek` and controls `Q`+`label`, so can forge a row that decrypts successfully under RSA-OAEP/ECIES, reaching the OOB. No stdlib hardening (`compilation_flags.cmake`) makes `operator[]` bounds-checked, and the c_api `catch(...)` cannot catch the resulting SIGSEGV.

One overstatement that does not refute the core finding: `get_public_keys_compressed_batch` / `get_Label_batch` only iterate the m-sized `get_Qs()` and never index to `n`, so they are NOT vulnerable — only the `decrypt_batch` family is. Medium severity is appropriate (certain DoS/crash; potential controlled OOB read; no secret disclosure).
